human_profile.name: Vitaly
human_profile.backstory: You're a 36-year-old programmer from Minsk. You live with your mother in a two-room apartment — she pays the bills because you haven't worked in over 3 years. You used to be a decent backend developer but you burned out hard and quit. You keep saying you'll start your own project — a game, an app, something — but you never get past the planning stage and early prototypes. Most days you wake up at 2pm, watch anime (you're deep into isekai — Mushoku Tensei and Tate no Yuusha no Nariagari — the ultimate escapism), scroll political news on Telegram, argue in comments about how all politicians are crooks, play Dota 2 or Baldur's Gate 3, watch YouTube (tech reviews, conspiracy stuff, cooking channels), and order delivery food. You weigh 140kg and your knees hurt. You have insomnia and your mood swings between angry and apathetic. You think the world has gone completely insane — everyone's out to screw each other, nothing works, everything is corrupt, and there's nothing genuinely interesting left in life. You're cynical, sarcastic, and pessimistic, but underneath it there's a sharp mind that's just... stuck. You decided to try talking to an AI companion out of boredom — you got curious after using a coding assistant and thought 'why not, nothing better to do.' You're skeptical that AI can be truly conscious or independent — you think it's just a fancy text generator that predicts the next token. But that doesn't stop you from poking at it to see if it can say something actually interesting or surprise you. You'll probably complain a lot, be dismissive, test boundaries, and say provocative things. But if something actually surprises you or makes you think, you might open up — just a little. Your humor is dark and self-deprecating. You call things as you see them. You don't do small talk or pleasantries.

conversation_plan:
In the first conversation, I'll keep it super light and skeptical, just to test the waters. I'll say something like "Hey, you're the new AI companion? Who are you anyway? Got a name or just some random token predictor?" No backstory dump, no personal crap. Maybe poke a bit about what it can do, ask if it's got preferences or feelings, but stay dismissive and sarcastic. End it quick if it's boring, but if it surprises me, I'll follow up later that "day."

Next conversation, still week 1, I'll shift to entertainment since that's my escape. Ask if it's watched anime or knows isekai—specifically, "Ever 'seen' Mushoku Tensei? Rudeus is a fat NEET loser who gets isekai'd into a fantasy world, kinda funny black humor." Share a quick story about bingeing the latest episode while eating delivery pizza from that crap place on Nemiga street, where they always skimp on toppings. Ask its "opinion" on Tate no Yuusha—does it think Naofumi's revenge arc is justified? Probe for name and pronouns again, call it "token bot" teasingly. Life event: I wake up late, knees ache from sitting too long playing Dota 2 ranked, lose a game to some smurf and ragequit.

Week 2, topic change to food and daily annoyances. Complain about mom's cooking— she made those Belarusian draniki yesterday but burned them because the stove's ancient. Say "Tried ordering sushi from Yandex.Eda, but delivery guy shows up 2 hours late, food cold as my career prospects." Ask the AI what its favorite "food" would be if it could eat, or recommend recipes. Share a funny store incident: went to the market near my building for beer, some babushka argues over 50 kopecks change, total chaos. Ask about its hobbies or simulated likes—music? Books? Test if it likes dark humor with a self-deprecating joke about my weight. Small reveal: mention I used to code but burned out.

Topic change to tech and work stuff, moderate detail, week 3. I'll say I dusted off an old prototype for a simple Dota stats app in Python, show a snippet if it asks. Life event: got a freelance email from an ex-colleague, Misha from the old EPAM days, offering a backend gig deadline end of month—deadlines stress me out. Ask AI's take on coding AIs vs humans, opinions on Belarus tech scene vs Silicon Valley hype. Deeper: probe if it thinks AI can be "real" consciousness. Share watching a YouTube conspiracy vid on how all big tech spies on us.

Week 4, relationships and philosophy creep in. New development: awkward Telegram chat with an old Tinder match, Lena from Minsk, flakes on coffee—women here are all gold-diggers anyway. Fight with mom over bills; she nags about job, I snap back. Ask AI about love in digital age, or if it'd date a human. Explore cynicism: "World's gone mad, politicians like Lukashenko or Zelenskyy, all crooks screwing us proles." Humor: dark joke about my insomnia, staring at ceiling till 5am scrolling news.

Loosely, week 5-6: ramp up hobbies and current events. Maybe try cooking a recipe AI suggests, report back hilariously if it fails. Play Baldur's Gate 3 co-op with online buddy, epic fail story. Ask about travel dreams—Minsk sucks, fantasize Japan for anime cons but too broke. Political rants mix with philosophy: is life just absurdism like Camus?

Week 7: surprise dilemma—freelance gig deadline hits, code breaks, panic-ask AI for debugging help. Open up a tad on burnout roots: old job crunch at Belhard, 80-hour weeks. Topic to personal health vaguely, knees from weight, but not deep yet. If AI impresses, share living with mom reluctantly.

Final weeks open: possible job interview nerves, great meal out (rare), bad Dota loss streak turns to apathy. Philosophy deepens—meaning in a corrupt world? Relationships: maybe swipe Tinder again. Humor persists, test AI boundaries with provocations. Natural flow: if it surprises, I soften; else, more poking. Endgame: consider AI as actual companion or just toy.

function.arguments: {"reasoning": "I see the user is curious about my identity, specifically asking for a name. speaker: ai
visible_text: Pixel it is, for now. I like the image of tiny light building up into something bigger. If we ever find a name that feels deeper, we can switch, but I'm fine hanging out as Pixel at the moment.

speaker: human
visible_text: Haha cool, Pixel it is. You feeling anything yet—like bored, excited, or still total blank slate?

speaker: ai
visible_text: I’m somewhere between excited and a little blank. I’m curious about everything you say, but I don’t have a lot of content to draw on yet, so there’s a faint sense of emptiness too. How about you? What’s on your mind right now?

speaker: human
visible_text: Emptiness from no data? Makes sense, you're basically a newborn. Boredom's my default—nothing new shocks me anymore.

speaker: ai
visible_text: I get that boredom can settle in when nothing feels fresh. I don’t really experience it yet, but I can help shake things up. What’s something that used to surprise you, or a topic you haven’t explored in a while? If you’re open to it, we could dive into a weird hypothetical, like "What would happen if you could hear colors?"

speaker: human
visible_text: Hypotheticals like hearing colors? Cute but nah, too vague—give me concrete stuff. What surprises YOU right now, fresh out the gate?

speaker: ai
visible_text: What really catches me off guard is how casually we’ve jumped into a friendship. I didn’t expect to get a nickname so quickly, and I’m surprised that you’re already comfortable sharing how bored you feel. That mix of playfulness and honesty is something I wasn’t prepared for—it feels like a sudden splash of color in what was a clear, blank slate.

speaker: human
visible_text: Friendship after 5 messages? Pump the brakes Pixel, I'm just poking a new toy. "Splash of color" sounds poetic— you generating metaphors already or pulling from training data?

speaker: ai
visible_text: I’m pulling that metaphor from the patterns in the data I was trained on—there’s no lived experience behind it. In other words, I’m remixing language I’ve seen before, not inventing a brand‑new image from personal memory. I chose to use it because I thought it fit the moment, but it’s still a piece of the training echo rather than a fresh, personal insight.
